content: The book of Obadiah records a vision from the LORD against which nation?
explanation: Obadiah 1:1 — 'The vision of Obadiah. Thus says the Lord GOD concerning Edom...' The entire book is a prophecy of judgment on the Edomites for their pride and violence against their kinsman Jacob.

content: What does the LORD declare He will make Edom among the nations?
explanation: Obadiah 1:2 — 'Behold, I will make you small among the nations; you shall be utterly despised.' God humbles Edom because of their pride.

content: According to Obadiah 1:3, what deceived the heart of Edom?
explanation: Obadiah 1:3 — 'The pride of your heart has deceived you, you who live in the clefts of the rock...' Edom's pride — fueled by their secure mountain stronghold — convinced them no one could bring them down.

content: Obadiah 1:4: Wherever Edom builds its nest, the LORD will still bring them down — what image does the verse use?
explanation: Obadiah 1:4 — 'Though you soar aloft like the eagle, though your nest is set among the stars, from there I will bring you down, declares the LORD.' The hyperbole stresses that no place is beyond God's reach.
options: Among the stars, soaring like the eagle
In the depths of the sea

content: Obadiah 1:5 uses thieves and grape gatherers to illustrate what about the judgment on Edom?
explanation: Obadiah 1:5 — 'If thieves came to you... would they not steal only enough for themselves? If grape gatherers came... would they not leave gleanings? How you have been destroyed!' Edom's plundering will be more thorough than any thief or gleaner — nothing will remain.
options: The enemy will spare them out of mercy
Edom will only lose a small portion
The plundering will be total — nothing left
They will keep their hidden treasures

content: What does Obadiah 1:6 say will happen to Esau's hidden treasures?
explanation: Obadiah 1:6 — 'How Esau has been pillaged, his treasures sought out!' Even Edom's most secret riches will be ransacked and taken.

content: According to Obadiah 1:7, who will betray Edom in their day of disaster?
explanation: Obadiah 1:7 — 'All your allies have driven you to your border; those at peace with you have deceived you... those who eat your bread have set a trap beneath you.' It is Edom's own allies and table companions who turn on them.
options: Their allies and those who ate their bread

content: Obadiah 1:8: On the day of judgment, what will the LORD destroy out of Edom?
explanation: Obadiah 1:8 — 'Will I not on that day, declares the LORD, destroy the wise men out of Edom, and understanding out of Mount Esau?' The wisdom Edom once boasted of will also be taken away by God.
options: The wise men and the men of understanding

content: Obadiah 1:9: What will become of the mighty men of Teman on that day?
explanation: Obadiah 1:9 — 'And your mighty men shall be dismayed, O Teman, so that every man from Mount Esau will be cut off by slaughter.' Even Edom's celebrated warriors will be terrified.
options: They will hide in the rocks
They will be dismayed and cut off
They will be rescued by the LORD

content: Obadiah 1:10: What is the direct reason Edom will be covered with shame and cut off forever?
explanation: Obadiah 1:10 — 'Because of the violence done to your brother Jacob, shame shall cover you, and you shall be cut off forever.' Edom's sin is violence against their own kin.
options: Refusing to pay tribute to God

content: Obadiah 1:11 condemns Edom for what attitude when foreigners carried Israel away?
explanation: Obadiah 1:11 — 'On the day that you stood aloof, on the day that strangers carried off his wealth and foreigners entered his gates and cast lots for Jerusalem, you were like one of them.' Edom stood by indifferently — complicit through silence.
options: They stood aloof, complicit with the enemy

content: In Obadiah 1:12-14, the LORD forbids Edom from doing which things to their brother Jacob in the day of disaster? (select all that apply)
explanation: Obadiah 1:12-14 — 'Do not gloat over the day of your brother... do not enter the gate of my people in the day of their calamity... do not stand at the crossroads to cut off his fugitives; do not hand over his survivors...' God lists Edom's actual sins: rejoicing over disaster, entering to plunder, blocking escape routes to hand over fugitives.
options: Gloat over your brother's day of misfortune
Enter the gate of God's people to plunder
Pray for the people of Israel
Stand at the crossroads to cut off those who escape

content: Obadiah 1:15: The day of the LORD is near upon whom?
explanation: Obadiah 1:15 — 'For the day of the LORD is near upon all the nations. As you have done, it shall be done to you; your deeds shall return on your own head.' The day of the LORD falls not only on Edom but on every nation.

content: What principle of judgment does Obadiah 1:15 lay down for the nations on the day of the LORD?
explanation: Obadiah 1:15 — '...As you have done, it shall be done to you; your deeds shall return on your own head.' Retributive justice: each will reap exactly what they sowed.
options: Each receives back what they have done to others

content: Obadiah 1:16: As Edom drank on God's holy mountain, what will the nations drink continually?
explanation: Obadiah 1:16 — 'For as you have drunk on my holy mountain, so all the nations shall drink continually; they shall drink and swallow, and shall be as though they had never been.' The cup of God's wrath — they drink until they are no more.
options: The cup of God's wrath, until they vanish

content: Obadiah 1:17: What will be on Mount Zion for God's people?
explanation: Obadiah 1:17 — 'But in Mount Zion there shall be those who escape, and it shall be holy, and the house of Jacob shall possess their own possessions.' Amid the judgment oracles, God promises rescue and restored inheritance for His people.
options: Deliverance — the mountain shall be holy, and Jacob shall possess his inheritance

content: What image does Obadiah 1:18 use to describe the house of Jacob over against the house of Esau?
explanation: Obadiah 1:18 — 'The house of Jacob shall be a fire, and the house of Joseph a flame, and the house of Esau stubble; they shall burn them and consume them...' Jacob is fire that consumes Esau the stubble.
options: House of Jacob is fire, house of Esau is stubble
House of Jacob is water, house of Esau is sand
House of Jacob is an eagle, house of Esau is vermin
House of Jacob is silver, house of Esau is gold

content: Obadiah 1:19: Which territory will those of the Negeb (south) possess?
explanation: Obadiah 1:19 — 'Those of the Negeb shall possess Mount Esau, and those of the Shephelah shall possess the land of the Philistines...' The Negev will take over Esau's own mountain — a striking historical reversal.

content: Obadiah 1:20: The exiles of the people of Israel will possess the land of the Canaanites — as far as where?
explanation: Obadiah 1:20 — 'The exiles of this host of the people of Israel shall possess the land of the Canaanites as far as Zarephath, and the exiles of Jerusalem who are in Sepharad shall possess the cities of the Negeb.' The post-exile return restores territory from Canaan all the way north to Zarephath.

content: Obadiah 1:21: To whom does the closing verse of the book declare the kingdom shall belong?
explanation: Obadiah 1:21 — 'Saviors shall go up to Mount Zion to rule Mount Esau, and the kingdom shall be the LORD's.' The book closes by proclaiming that ultimate kingship belongs to the LORD.
